title: THE REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
THE REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEAL TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI REGISTRY
TAX APPEAL NO. 1367 OF 2022
TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED ……….….……………
APPELLANT
-VERSUS-
COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES …………………………
RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a private limited company incorporated in Kenya.
Its principal activity is the manufacture and sale of sugar.
2. The Respondent is a principal officer appointed under Section 13
of the Kenya Revenue Authority Act, CAP 469 of the Laws of Kenya
(hereinafter “the Act”). Under Section 5 (1) of the Act, the Kenya
Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government for collecting
and receiving all tax revenue. Further, under Section 5(2) of the
Act, concerning the performance of its functions under subsection
(1), the Authority is mandated to administer and enforce all
provisions of the written laws as set out in Parts 1 & 2 of the First
Schedule to the Act to assess, collect and account for all revenues
under those laws.
4. The dispute arose when the Appellant applied for an extension of
the period to carry forward unutilized losses incurred in the
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 1

financial years 2011 and 2012 beyond the stipulated 10 years as
prescribed in Section 15(5) (now repealed) of the Income Tax Act,
CAP 470 of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter the “ITA”).
5. The Appellant’s application was made vide a letter dated 24th July
2019 to carry forward a tax deficit of Kshs. 1,840,023,570 as of the
end of the 2011 financial year.
6. That the being aware that Section 15(5) of the ITA that it was
relying on had been repealed, the Appellant sought a private
ruling vide a letter dated 26th October 2021, on whether it could
carry forward these losses as was hitherto provided in the ITA.
7. The Respondent issued its Private Ruling vide a letter dated 26th
November 2021 where it counselled that only unexpired losses as
of the date of the change of the law, 1st July 2021, shall be subject
to the new law.
8. Subsequent thereto, the Appellant wrote letters dated 24th July
2019 and 5th October 2020 seeking an extension of the period to
carry forward the deficits in issue.
9. The Respondent issued its response in a letter dated 29th
September 2022 where it stated that the Cabinet Secretary had
approved an extension period to carry forward unutilised losses of
Kshs 774,809,156.00 for the 2011 financial year.
10. Dissatisfied with this response, the Appellant filed TAT No. 1367
of 2022: Transmara Sugar Company Limited vs.
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes wherein the TAT through a
Judgement delivered on 24th November 2024, made a finding of
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 2

fact and law that the impugned decision before it was that of the
CS Treasury and not KRA’s and that therefore, it lacked jurisdiction
to determine the appeal, thereby dismissing the same.
11. Dissatisfied with the Judgement of the TAT, the Appellant appealed
to the High Court in HCCOM ITA/E009 of 2024: Transmara
Sugar Company Limited vs. Commissioner of Domestic
Taxes. The High court vide a judgement delivered on 28th October,
2025 set aside the decision of the Tribunal and remitted the
matter to the Tribunal for hearing and determination on merits, in
accordance with the provisions of the Tax Procedures Act, CAP
469B of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “TPA”), and with due
regard to the reasons and computations already furnished by the
Respondent pursuant to Section 51(10) of the TPA.
THE APPEAL
12. The Appellant’s Memorandum of Appeal which was dated and filed
on the 11th of November 2022 was premised on the following
grounds:
(a) That Respondent erred in law and fact by denying the
Appellant its right to enjoy the incentive for investment
deduction, which incentive is provided in law for purposes of
encouraging and rewarding investment in Kenya.
(b) The Respondent misdirected itself in law and in fact by failing
to give its reasons in the decision dated 29th September 2022
contrary to the provision of Section 49 of the TPA.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 3

(c) That the Respondent erred in law and fact, in its decision
dated 29 September 2022 by finding the decision on an
erroneous and unprocedural assessment.
(d) That it was not open in law for the Respondent to arrive at its
decision dated 29th September 2022 without affording the
Appellant a right to fair administrative action contrary to the
dictates of Section 3(b)and (g) of the Fair Administrative
Actions Act, CAP 7L of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “FAAA”).
(e) That the Respondent erred in fact and law in its decision dated
29 September 2022 and the attendant email of 15th October
2022 by failing to give due regard to the reasons and the
evidence submitted by the Appellant in its application dated
24th July 2019.
APPELLANT’S CASE
13. The Appellant has supported its Appeal with its Statement of Facts
dated and filed on the 11th November 2022 the testimony of Mr.
Joseph Ringui dated and filed on the 8th May 2023 that was
admitted in evidence on oath on the 28th September, 2023 and
also filed written submissions dated 13th September 2023 and filed
on the 18th October 2023.
14. The Appellant’s case was premised on the following facts:
(a) That the denial of this extension was unjustified because it
had complied with all the conditions prescribed under Section
15(5) (repealed) of the ITA.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 4

(b) The Respondent's decision dated 29th September 2022
contravened Article 47(2) of the Constitution, of Kenya, 2010
(hereinafter “the Constitution”) Section 49 of the TPA and
Section 3(d) of the FAAA as it did not embody reasons for the
decision. It supported this assertion with the case of Republic
v Retirement Benefits Appeals Tribunal and 5 others
Ex-parte Kenya Airports Authority staff superannuation
scheme [2025] eKLR.
(c) The impugned decision was based on error, because the
Respondent had used erroneous tax periods and budgeted
sugar production figures instead of the actual sugar produced,
in arriving at its conclusion. That this irregularity resulted in
an irregular assessment.
(d) The Respondent arrived at its decision without allowing it the
chance to be heard contrary to Article 50(1) of the
Constitution. It supported this view with the case of JMK v
MWM and Another [2015] eKLR.
(e) The reasons it provided in support of its application were not
considered when the Respondent arrived at its decision
contrary to Article 47 of the Constitution and Section 5(1) (b)
and (c) of the FAAA. It supported this position with the case of
Nizaba International Trading Company Limited v Kenya
Revenue Authority.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 5

(f) Its legitimate expectation that it would be allowed to utilize its
losses incurred in the 2011 and 2012 financial years was
breached contrary to the settled principles in the case of
Keroche Industries Limited v Kenya Revenue Authority
and 5 Others Nairobi HCMA No. 743 of 2006 [2007] KLR
240.
15. It identified the following as the issues falling for determination in
this Appeal:
(a) Whether this Tribunal is clothed with jurisdiction to hear and
determine this Appeal.
(b) Whether the Respondent misdirected itself in law and fact by
failing to give reasons for its decision dated 29 th September
2022 thereby partially invalidating the decision.
(c) Whether the Respondent’s decision dated 29 th September
2022 is partially invalid by dint of it being arrived at based on
an erroneous and unprocedural tax assessment contrary to
Section 3(b) and (g) of the Fair Administrative Act, 2015.
(d) Whether the Respondent’s decision dated 29th September
2022 is partially defective for being founded on factual
inaccuracies, erroneous and unprocedural tax assessment
contrary to the Tax Procedures Act.
(e) Whether the Respondent erred in law and fact by denying the
Appellant its right to enjoy the incentive for investment
deduction, which incentive is provided in law for purposes of
encouraging, promoting and rewarding investments in Kenya.
16. It argued each issue as hereunder:
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 6

(a) Whether this Tribunal is clothed with jurisdiction to hear and
determine this Appeal.
17. The Appellant stated that the impugned decision was made by the
Respondent and not the Cabinet Secretary, Treasury for the
following reasons:
(i) The Respondent having made this assertion bore the burden
of proof to persuade this Tribunal that indeed this was not its
decision but a communication of the CS Treasury. Regrettably,
the Respondent did not supply any evidence to prove that
they were under instructions to communicate the decision of
the CS Treasury.
(ii) It is trite law that the burden of proof lies on he who alleges a
fact. Section 107 of the Evidence Act, CAP 80 of the Laws of
Kenya provides as follows:
“ (1) Whoever desires any court to give judgment as to any
legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which
he asserts must prove that those facts exist"
(iii) During the hearing of this matter in the open Tribunal, the
Appellant's witness Mr. Joseph Ringui did point out in re-
examination, whilst referring to the decision dated 29th
September 2022, that it was apparent that this decision was
on the letterhead of the Kenya Revenue Authority which
confirmed that this was the Respondent's decision and not
any other person's. That the reference number of the said
decision was KRA/DTD/P&TA/1015/24 and there wasn’t any
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 7

other reference number letter from treasury. In its view this
reference number was an assurance that this was a decision
of the Respondent.
(iv) The Appellant's witness also attested during his re-
examination that the letter was signed by: "Ms Esther
Wahome; Deputy Commissioner-Policy & Tax Advisory” who
appended her signature thereon NOT on behalf of the CS
Treasury, but rather: “FOR COMMISSIONER FOR DOMESTIC
TAXES”;
(v) In the absence of evidence to support the Respondent's
allegations, there was therefore little doubt as to whose
decision the letter of 29th September 2022 was. Further, it
was common practice for the Treasury to render its decisions
on its letterhead and by the designated officials. Nothing
would have been easier than for the CS Treasury to sign off a
letter on the CS Treasury's letterhead, directing or instructing
the Respondent of its decision, which decision would then be
conveyed by the Respondent concerning that communication.
In the absence of this communication, the Respondent's
allegations were mere allegations made with a view of
circumventing the Appellant's right to access justice by way of
this Appeal. By its conduct, the Respondent was estopped
from denying authorship and ownership of the content of its
letter dated 29 September 2022.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 8

(vi) The Appellant cited Section 15(5) of the ITA (now repealed),
which stated as follows:
“(5) Notwithstanding section (4), the minister may on the
recommendation of the commissioner, extend the period of
deduction beyond ten years where a person applies through
the commissioner for such extension, giving evidence of
inability to extinguish the deficit within that period”
18. It submitted that the Appellant did not follow the procedure laid
down in Section 15(5) of the ITA and argued that whereas it made
its application through the Respondent, the decision was issued by
the Respondent and not the CS Treasury. That it was thus justified
to sue the Respondent who had issued the said decision. It relied
on the case of Vivo Energy Kenya Limited v Commissioner of
Customs & Border Control, Kenya Revenue Authority &
Another (2020) eKLR to support that argument.
19. It was also its view, that there was nothing on record to
demonstrate that the Respondent had made recommendations to
the CS Treasury and that the CS Treasury was the owner of the
decision in dispute herein.
20. The Appellant stated that the erroneous decision by the CS
Treasury emanated from an erroneous rationalization by the
Respondent with no evidence of CS Treasury's intervention. That
the detailed reasons for the decision which was vide an electronic
mail dated 14th October 2022 were also conveyed by the
Respondent and not the CS Treasury and hence the reason why it
holds that the impugned decision was issued by the Respondent.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 9

21. The Appellant invoked the principle of ‘delagatus non potest
delegare’ to state that a person to whom a power, trust, or
authority is given to act on behalf or for the benefit of another,
cannot delegate this obligation unless expressly authorized to do
so by the law, and that Section 15(5) of the ITA had placed these
exclusive powers only on the CS Treasury.
22. The Appellant stated that it was improper for the Respondent to
claim that it was undertaking the role of the CS Treasury when in
fact, there was no Gazette Notice or any proof that those powers
had been delegated to the Respondent by the CS Treasury. It
relied on the case of Republic v University of Nairobi Ex Parte
Maxwel Magawi Odhiambo (2019) eKLR, to explain how the
“delagatus non potest delgare” principle works.
23. The Appellant stated that the impugned decision was appealable
because it was a decision of the CS Treasury communicated by the
Respondent (as claimed by the Respondent). That it is a decision
made under a tax law under Section 15(5) (repealed) of the ITA
and it is thus an appealable decision, in which case it was properly
filed before the Tribunal.
24. The Appellant averred that the said decision did not fit with the
definition of a tax decision in Section 3 of the TPA. That it instead
fell within the ambit of Section 2 of the TPA which has included ITA
as one of the statutes falling under the definition of tax law.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 10

(b) Whether the Respondent misdirected itself in law and fact by
failing to give reasons for its decision dated 29 th September 2022
thereby partially invalidating the decision.
22. The Appellant posited that the decision dated 29th September 2022
did not provide a statement of reasons to allow it to know the
reasons for the Respondent's decision contrary to Article 47 of the
constitution, Section 3(d) of the FAA Act and Section 49 of the TPA.
It relied on the case Republic v Retirement Benefits Appeals
Tribunal & 5 Others Ex-Parte Kenya Airports Authority Staff
Superannuation Scheme (2015) eKLR to stress this point.
(c) Whether the Respondent’s decision dated 29 th September 2022 is
partially invalid by dint of it being arrived at based on an
erroneous and unprocedural tax assessment contrary to Section
3(b) and (g) of the Fair Administrative Act, 2015.
23. The Appellant postulated that the Respondent failed to consider
the information and documents presented for its consideration
contrary to the dictates of Article 47 of the Constitution and
Section 5 (1) (b) and (c) of the FAAA and that the reasons for the
decision were irregular as it came after the impugned decision had
been issued on the 14th of October 2022 and only after the same
had been requested by the Appellant.
24. The Appellant affirmed that the Respondent denied its request and
instead relied on extraneous reasons provided by Kenya Sugar
Board without engaging the Appellant contrary to principles of
natural justice and the dictates of Articles 47 and 50 of the
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 11

Constitution. It also placed reliance on the case of JKM v WMM &
another (2015) eKLR.
25. The Appellant submitted that it tendered its application on 24th July
2019 and obtained a response thereto on 29th September 2022.
That this delay of over 3 years in rendering the decision was
inordinate and highly prejudicial. It cited the case of Ericsson
Kenya Limited v Attorney General & 3 other (2014) eKLR, to
argue that the Respondent ought to have issued its decision within
a reasonable time.
(d) Whether the Respondent’s Decision dated 29 th September 2022 is
partially defective for being founded on factual inaccuracies,
erroneous and unprocedural tax assessment contrary to the Tax
Procedures Act.
26. The Appellant averred that the reasons on which the impugned
decision was based were founded on an unprocedural assessment
which contravened Section 31 of the TPA as it was not involved or
notified of the decision before it was issued. That the said
assessment was thus improper as was stated in the case of
Republic v Kenya Revenue Authority (KRA) & 4 others; New
Flamingo Hardware & Paints Limited & 22 others (Ex Parte)
[2020] eKLR and Geothermal Development Company
Limited v Attorney General & 3 others (2013) eKLR.
27. It submitted that the Respondent relied on an erroneous tax
period, bagged sugar and also relied on estimated budget figures
to arrive at its erroneous decision.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 12

(e) Whether the Respondent erred in law and fact by denying the
Appellant its right to enjoy the incentive for investment deduction,
which incentive is provided in law for purposes of encouraging,
promoting and rewarding investments in Kenya.
28. The Appellant invoked the doctrine of legitimate expectation to
argue that the law and practice and entitled it to carry forward its
unutilised losses and that this was breached in this case contrary
to the court guidance in Keroche Industries Ltd v Kenya
Revenue Authority and 5 others Nairobi HCMA No. 743 of
2006 [2007] KLR 240 and that it had the right to certainty and
predictability in the applicability of conduct, rules, policies and
procedures which underlie the proper regulation of economic
activities and in this case the treatment of losses.
29. The Appellant in concluding stated that the Respondent had
generally not properly exercised its discretion when it denied it the
right to carry forward its unutilised losses on the basis that it did
not qualify for the extension.
Appellant’s Prayer
30. Arising from the above the Appellant prayed for the following
orders:
(a) That the Appeal is allowed.
(b) The Tribunal be pleased to seize jurisdiction under Section
29(3) (c) (i) of the Tax Tribunal Act, CAP 469A of the Laws of
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 13

Kenya (hereinafter “TATA”) and to consider the evidence and
submissions made in connection hereto and to find that
because the decision to disallow the carry forward of the tax
losses in issue was founded on an erroneous and unprocedural
assessment, the same ought to be set aside.
(c) That the erroneous assessment contained in the electronic
mail dated 15th October 2022 be set aside, and that the
Tribunal do find that there were no grounds for the Appellant
to not be entitled to carry forward the disallowed tax losses in
the sum of Kshs. 1,065,214,414.00, concerning the year 2011
and the Tribunal effectively allow the Appellant to extend the
utilization of the tax losses.
That in the Alternative to prayer (b) and (c) above:
(d) The Tribunal be pleased to direct the Respondent to grant the
Appellant’s Application dated 24th July 2012 within 60 days
from the date of the judgment hereof, in compliance with the
provisions of the law.
(e) Any other relief that the Tribunal may deem fit to grant.
(f)The cost and incidental to this Appeal be awarded.
RESPONDENT’S CASE
31. The Respondent has grounded its case on its Statement of Facts
dated and filed on the 1st December 2022 and filed written
submissions dated 24th October 2023.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 14

32. The Respondent stated that it had requested the Appellant to
submit evidence supporting the losses and evidence supporting its
inability to recoup the losses within the 10 years starting in 2011
but it failed to do so.It further submitted that it carried out a data
analysis of the Appellant’s sugar production and sales for the
period 2011 to 2021 before the review was issued wherein it
recommended to the CS Treasury for approval of Kshs.
774,809,156.00. That the CS Treasury approved the request as it
had recommended.
33. The Respondent took the position that it followed the laid down
procedure in reviewing the application by the Appellant for an
extension of the period to carry forward losses beyond 10 years
and it did not deny the Appellant its right to enjoy the incentive for
investment deduction as provided in law. That all the valid capital
allowance claims were allowed when arriving at the decision
recommended to the Minister as required in law.
34. The Respondent further averred that the adjustments made to the
losses before the recommendations were based on other
observations regarding the Appellant's income declarations. That
no capital allowances were disallowed in arriving at the
recommendation that the Respondent made to the Minister
regarding the Appellant’s application.
35. The Respondent submitted that it was not required to
communicate its recommendation to the Appellant before making
a recommendation to the Minster concerning the application for
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 15

carry forward of losses and that it had forwarded all the
documents submitted by the Appellant together with its
recommendation to the Minister for consideration.
36. The Respondent affirmed that the decision was not a refusal but a
recommendation to the Minister following the law under Section
15(5)[now repealed] of the ITA and took the position that its letter
dated 29th September 2022 was not its decision but was instead a
communication of the Minister’s approval of the Appellant’s
request to carry forward the losses in accordance with the
Appellant’s Constitutional right and right to fair administrative
action. The Respondent further stated that it considered all
reasons and evidence by the Appellant in arriving at its
recommendation to the Minister.
37. The Respondent averred that the letter dated 29th September
2022 was not an assessment as alleged by the Appellant but a
communication of the approval by the Minister based on the
Appellant’s application to carry forward losses beyond 10 years.
That the said recommendation is not appealable to the Tribunal
under the meaning of appealable decision in Section 3 of the ITA,
Section 52 of the TPA and the TATA. it relied on the case of Mount
Kenya Bottlers Limited and 3 others vs The Attorney
General and 3 Others (2019) eKLR to support this argument.
38. The Respondent was of the view that the absence of an appealable
decision in this Appeal implies that the Tribunal lacks the
jurisdiction to entertain it. In which case it ought to down its tools
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 16

as was stated in the case of Owners of Motor Vessel “Lilian S”
v Caltex Oil (Kenya) Ltd [1989] eKLR. this suit The
Respondent concluded by stating that the Appellant had not
exhausted all avenues of dispute resolution as envisaged in the
TATA.
Respondent’s Prayers
39. Based on the above grounds the Respondent sought the following
reliefs:
(a) That the Tribunal lacks jurisdiction to entertain the case when
no decision has been issued.
(b) That it be awarded costs of the Appeal.
PARTIES FURTHER SUBMISSIONS
40. The Tribunal granted parties leave to file further submissions
following the fresh hearing conducted in compliance with the
orders of the High Court in HCCOM ITA/E009 of 2024:
Transmara Sugar Company Limited vs. Commissioner of
Domestic Taxes:
Appellant’s Further Submissions
41. The Appellant relied on its further submissions dated and filed on
24th February 2026 wherein it submitted that the Respondent’s
Decision fell in error because of the following reasons, in
summary:
(a) Failure to appreciate the change in the year end for 2018;
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 17

(b) Misapplying the Kenya Sugar Board Data: By relying on the
figure of 67,040 tonnes 12 months 2018 calendar year when
in fact, it ought to have considered sugar bagged for 6 months
declared half financial year as per the sugar board data which
was 29,836 tonnes;
(c) Misinterpreting the Management account data: By applying
the figure of 52,699 tonnes which was the budget figure
instead of applying the actual sugar produced of 29,976
tonnes as per management accounts;
(d) Applying an erroneous average sugar price: By using the
figure of Kshs. 74,278 per tonne as the average sugar price,
without any basis whatsoever, when in fact the ascertained
average sugar price was Kshs. 64,904 per tonne; and
(e) Imposition of Income Tax on budget figures: The Respondent
treated budgeted or projected figures as though they
constituted taxable income. Tax is levied on income earned or
accrued within the meaning of the ITA, not on projections or
budgetary estimates. By using figures that were not realised
into taxable income, the Respondent wrongly distorted the
Appellant’s true tax position.
42. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent used the wrong
figures and came to an incorrect finding. That the assessment fails
the test of accuracy, legality, and reasonableness and ought to be
set aside in its entirety and that that the principles governing such
conduct were clearly articulated in Fleur Investments Limited v
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 18

Commissioner of Domestic Taxes & another (2018) eKLR,
where it was held as follows:
“25. Therefore, whereas this court is not entitled to question the
merits of the decision of the taxing authority, that authority must
exercise its powers fairly and there ought to be a basis for the
exercise of such powers. A taxing authority is not entitled to pluck
a figure from the air and impose it upon a taxpayer without some
rational basis for arriving at that figure and not another figure.
Such action would be arbitrary, capricious and in bad faith. It
would be an unreasonable exercise of power and discretion that
would justify the court in intervening.”
43. The Appellant submitted further that, it was apparent that the
Respondent was guilty for not properly applying its mind on the
data that it was provided for, its decision being a quasi-judicial
decision, a concern that this Tribunal has hitherto raised in the
case of Digital Box Ltd vs. Commissioner of Domestic
Taxes (2017) eKLR, where the following reasoning in Van
Boeckel vs. C & E QB 1980 STC 290 in respect of “judgement”
was adopted:
“104. the very use of the word ‘judgement’ makes it clear that
the commissioners are required to exercise their powers in such a
way that they make a value judgement on the material which is
before them.
…What the words “best judgement” envisage, in my view, is that
the commissioners will fairly consider all material placed before
them and on that material, come to a decision which is one which
is reasonable and amount of tax which is due.”
44. The Appellant submitted that in the process of making its
impugned decision, the Respondent did not engage with the
Appellant, leading to misunderstanding the data it relied upon to
arrive at its decision. That the Respondent acted unprocedurally
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 19

by denying the Appellant the right to be heard and hence arrived
at an erroneous conclusion, as evidenced by the erroneous
reasons given for the Respondent’s impugned decision.
45. The Appellant submitted that the Latin maxim audi alteram
partem (meaning "hear the other side") is a cornerstone of natural
justice. It requires that before any decision adversely affecting a
person's rights is taken, the affected party must be given a fair
opportunity to present their case. Whereas the Respondent made
some contact with the Appellant asking for some data, it failed or
refused to engage with the Appellant to be guided on the
operational meaning of the data obtained by the Respondent,
hence why they misdirected themselves and arrived at an
erroneous conclusion.
46. The Appellant submitted that Article 47 of the Constitution of
Kenya, 2010, guarantees that every person has the right to
administrative action that is expeditious, efficient, lawful,
reasonable, and procedurally fair. Where a person's right or
fundamental freedom is likely to be adversely affected by
administrative action, that person has the right to be given written
reasons for the action.
47. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent purely grounded its
defense on the premise that it was allegedly not the decision
makers of the impugned decision, but that it was merely
‘communicating’ the Minister’s decision. The High Court having
found that there was no evidence whatsoever of the
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 20

communication having come from the Minister, the rationale for
the rebuttal would have to come from the Respondent, yet there is
no substantive response to the arguments raised by the Appellant
in its substantive grounds of appeal.
48. The Appellant submitted that it is now settled law that unless a
party controverts, or traverses any averments made in pleadings,
silence, amounts to an admission. Certainly, the Court of Appeal
asserted in the case of Choitram & another v Nazari (Civil
Appeal 8 of 1982) [1984] KECA 47 (KLR) (20 January 1984)
where the Court held thus;_
“4. Each party is, therefore, required by the foregoing rules to deal
specifically with every allegation of fact the truth of which he does
not admit. Rule 9(2) requires him to make his traverse by a denial
eg “I deny” or by a statement of non-admission eg: "I do not
admit”, either expressly or by necessary implication. The court
should in an application for judgment on admissions examine the
pleadings carefully to see whether there are no specific denials or
no definite refusals to admit.”
49. The Appellant also relied on the case of Law Society of Kenya v
Attorney General & 2 others (2022) eKLR, where the Court
held that simplistic responses given by public authorities in the
guise of being reasons for a decision are not adequate and do not
meet the threshold prescribed in law.The Appellant submitted that
the Respondent has the power and authority to validate a request
for carry forward of tax losses. However, these powers must not be
exercised arbitrarily. That the principle of discretion is a
fundamental tenet of administrative law and is deeply entrenched
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 21

in Kenyan jurisprudence. That in Sharp v Wakefield [1891] AC
173 (House of Lords), it was held as follows:
"Discretion means when it is said that something is to be done
according to the rules of reason and justice, not according to
private opinion; according to law and not humour. It is to be, not
arbitrary, vague and fanciful, but legal and regular. And it must be
exercised within the limit, to which an honest man competent to
the discharge of his office ought to confine himself."
50. That further, in Kenya Revenue Authority & 2 Others v
Darasa Investments Limited [2018] eKLR, the Court of Appeal
held that whenever a court is called upon to interfere with the
exercise of judicial discretion, it ought not to interfere unless it is
satisfied that the decision-maker misdirected itself in some matter
and as a result arrived at a wrong decision, or that it is manifest
from the case as a whole that the decision-maker was wrong in the
exercise of discretion and occasioned injustice.
51. The Appellant submitted that in this instance, the Tribunal should
interfere and exercise its judicial discretion as the decision-maker,
the Respondent in this case, misdirected itself in the matter and
consequently arrived at a wrong decision.
Respondent’s Further Submissions
52. The Respondent relied on its further submissions dated and filed
on 24th February, 2026 wherein it identified the issues for
determination in this matter to be: -
(a) Whether the matter is properly before the Tribunal;
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 22

(b) Whether the Respondent was justified in issuing the
communication dated 29 th September, 2022.
53. The Respondent submitted that without prejudice to the judgment
of the High Court and the arguments made before this Tribunal on
the issue of jurisdiction, the Respondent still maintains that the
present matter is not properly before the Tribunal, but this time on
the basis of the fact that if indeed the communication was an
appealable decision, the Appellant ought to have followed the
regular procedure for objection the Commissioner's tax decisions
under Section 51 of the TPA. That accordingly, the Tribunal has no
jurisdiction to hear and determine the dispute as the suit is
premature.
54. The Respondent submitted that in the first instance, it is
undisputed that the Appellant in the present appeal, is seeking to
carry forward losses beyond the 10- year period starting from
2011 in the sum of Kshs. 1,840,023,570.00 which process was
guided by the provisions of Section 15 of the ITA as it then was.
55. The Respondent submitted that Section 15(5) of the ITA, (now
repealed) did not confer any powers on it to make a decision
regarding the Application by the Appellant as the same is the
preserve of the CS, National Treasury, which decision was
communicated by the Respondent in the impugned
communication of 29th September 2022.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 23

56. The Respondent asserted that the Appellant failed to exhaust all
avenues as required by the TPA. That Section 52 of the TPA,
Section 12 of the TATA and Sections 7 and 9(2) of the FAAA and
Article 159 (2) (c) of the Constitution of Kenya all provide for an
exhaustive dispute resolution mechanism for tax disputes.The
Respondent submitted that if at all the Appellant was dissatisfied
with the communication under challenge, it was meant to follow
the procedure under Section 51 of the TPA, by lodging an
objection, to wit the Respondent would consider and issue a
decision that would then trigger the Tribunal's jurisdiction to
review the Respondent's decision.
57. The Respondent submitted that the Tribunal in this case, is called
upon to apply strict interpretation of the law with no room for
intendment as was held in the case of Cape Brandy Syndicate
as cited with approval in the Court of Appeal case of Mount
Kenya Bottlers Limited & 3 Others v The Attorney General
and 3 others
58. With regard to the doctrine of exhaustion the respondent also
relied on the cases of:
 Mutanga Tea & Coffee Company Limited v Shikara
Limited & another [2015] eKLR
 William Odhiambo Ramogi & 3 others v Attorney
General & 4 others; Muslims for Human Rights & 2
others (Interested Parties) [2020] e KLR.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 24

 Speaker of National Assembly v Karume [ 1992] KLR
21
 Owners of the Motor Vessel "Lillian S" v Caltex Oil
(Kenya) Limited [ 1989] KLR 1.
59. The Respondent submitted that the Appellant in failing to follow
the laid-out procedures in the law, had not properly invoked the
jurisdiction of the Tribunal as it ought to appeal through an
objection, and thus the Appeal should be struck out for want of
jurisdiction.
60. The Respondent submitted that it reviewed the Appellant's
application to carry forward its losses beyond the 10-year period
starting from 2011 and established that the said loss arose from
capital deductions from substantive investments in plant and
machinery in the sum of Kshs. 1,840,023,570.00.
61. The Respondent submitted that upon analyzing the Appellant's
financial statements and third-party information such as records
from the Kenya Sugar Board, it established the Appellant had
under declared its taxable sales in the year of 2018 by Kshs
1,065,214,414.00. This figure the Respondent submits, was
arrived at by comparing the bagged sugar produced as per the
Appellant's report as compared with the figures from the Kenya
Sugar Board and multiplying the difference against the average
price per ton of sugar for the year 2018 according to the
Appellant's data.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 25

62. The Respondent submitted thus, in accordance to the provisions of
Section 15 (5) of the ITA recommended for the approval by the
Minister, the sum of Kshs. 774,809,156.00.
63. The Respondent submitted that at the hearing, the Appellant's
witness was at a loss to prove that there was an error in the data
relied upon. In fact, the witness admitted that no law required the
Respondent to only consider the information for part of the year
being the variance between the declared loss and the adjusted
loss to account for the under declared sales.
64. It was the Respondent's position that the carrying forward of
losses under the ITA, contrary to the Appellant's assertions is not
pegged on the financial year but rather the meaning of 'year' as
defined under the ITA which is defined as a calendar year. That
Section 2 of the Income Tax Act, defines year of income" as the
period of twelve months commencing on 1st January in any year
and ending on 31st December in that year.
65. It was the Respondent's submission that at all material times, it
acted in accordance with the stipulated law and thus did not
deprive the Appellant its right to enjoy the investment deduction
but rather it did review and allowed all the valid capital allowance
claims before recommending the resultant figure to the Minister.
66. Additionally, the Respondent submitted that the law as set out in
the above-mentioned Section 15(5) of the ITA does not place any
legal obligation on it to pre-communicate its recommendation to
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 26

the Appellant before making the recommendation to the Minister.
Accordingly, it would be absurd to suggest that this action
amounted to a rejection/ refusal of the Application or that the
same created a legitimate expectation with the Appellant.
67. The Respondent submitted that it is thus evident that, the decision
to extend is not made by the Respondent, but rather by the
Cabinet Secretary upon the Respondent's recommendation, who is
at liberty to accept or reject the recommendation.
68. That in any event, the Respondent maintains that it is not in
dispute and indeed as evidenced by the various correspondence
on the record, that the parties herein partook in several
engagements where records were exchanged and that culminated
in the impugned communication. The Respondent thus submits
that it did not infringe on the Appellant's constitutional rights to
fair hearing as alleged or at all.
69. The Respondent submitted that it wholly relied on the records

support of its application of 24th July 2019, as well as any reasons
and/ or evidence submitted of inability to recoup the losses over
the period of 10 years, before arriving at its recommendation. That
from the evidence on record, it is evident that the Respondent
relied on inter alia management accounts / KPls submitted by the
Appellant in computing under declared income.
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 27

70. That the Appellant through its witness at the hearing of the main
suit, further admitted that whenever it sought any clarification
from the Respondent, the same was promptly given.
71. The Respondent maintained that the impugned communication in
question was thus not an assessment but rather a communication
of the Minister decision with regard to the Appellant's application
to extend time for carrying forward losses after the stipulated 10-
year period. That it is the Respondent's humble submission
therefore that neither this Tribunal nor the Respondent can grant
the extension sought in the present suit, as the same is the sole
mandate of the Cabinet Secretary, as prescribed in the
aforementioned law.
72. In any event, the Respondent submits that in the present matter,
the Appellant failed to discharge its burden of proof in
demonstrating its inability to recoup and or extinguish the losses
claimed, necessitating the application for the extension of the 10-
year period.
73. The Respondent submitted that it acted in its best judgment with
all the available information in making its recommendation to the
Cabinet Secretary as required in law. That Section 56 (1) of the
TPA provides that in any proceedings that relate to tax decisions,
objections and Appeals, the burden shall be on the taxpayer to
prove that a tax decision is incorrect and that further, Section 30
the TATA provides as follows:
"In a proceeding before the Tribunal, the appellant has the burden
of proving-
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 28

where an appeal relates to an assessment, that the assessment is
excessive; or
in any other case, that the tax decision should not have been
made or should have been made differently."
74. The Respondent relied on the case of Commissioner of
Domestic Taxes v Trical and Hard Limited (Tax Appeal No.
E146 of 2020) [2022] KEHC 9927 (KLR) (Commercial and Tax),
where the Courts have stated that the Respondent has a
rebuttable presumption of correctness of its decisions.
75. The Respondent further relied on the case of Commissioner of
Domestic Taxes v Galaxy Tools Limited [2021] KEHC 5530
(KLR) on the burden of proof placed on the taxpayers as a result
of the self-assessment taxation regime in Kenya and thus the
Respondent is granted wide discretion in ascertaining after the
fact, what the correct taxable income is in any situation.
76. The Respondent stated that as already submitted earlier, it relied
on records furnished by the Appellant itself, a fact not in dispute.
The Respondent therefore maintained that the impugned
communication was made after consideration of all information
provided by the Appellant and in pursuant to the law.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
77. The issue of whether the Tribunal lacks jurisdiction to hear and
determine this Appeal on merits was settled by the High court vide
its judgement in HCCOM ITA/E009 of 2024: Transmara Sugar
Company Limited vs. Commissioner of Domestic Taxes
where it set aside the Tribunal’s finding that it did not have
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 29

jurisdiction to hear and determine the matter. The High Court
referred the Appeal back to the Tribunal for determination on its
merits.
78. The Tribunal will thus deal with the following singular issue:
Whether the Respondent was justified in issuing its decision as
contained in the letter dated 29th September 2022.
ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS
79. The background in this Appeal was that the Appellant made an
application dated 24th July 2019 for approval to carry forward
unutilized losses amounting to Kshs. 1,840,023,570.00 in relation
to financial year 2011 pursuant to the repealed Section 15(5) of
the ITA. The Respondent vide a letter dated the 29th September
2022 approved the carry forward of unutilized losses amounting to
Kshs. 774,809,156.00 and disallowed the extension of time to
carry forward losses amounting to Kshs. 1,065,214,414.00 for the
year 2011.
80. The Appellant contended that the said letter did not provide
reasons for the disallowance of losses of Kshs. 1,065,214,414.00
and that the reasons were only provided via email on 15th October,
2022 after it requested.
81. It is not in dispute that it is within the province of the Respondent
to recommend to the Cabinet Secretary on losses that merit to be
carried forward. The applicable law which provided for the carrying
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 30

forward of unutilized losses was pursuant to the following
provisions of Section 15(5) (now repealed) of the ITA:
“Notwithstanding section (4), the minister may on the
recommendation of the commissioner, extend the period of
deduction beyond ten years where a person applies through the
commissioner for such extension, giving evidence of inability to
extinguish the deficit within that period”
82. The dispute is in relation to disallowing of extension of time to
carry forward losses amounting to Kshs. 1,065,214,414.00 on the
Respondent’s recommendation. The impugned letter dated 29th
September 2022 in response to the Appellant’s application to carry
forward losses reads as follows:
“We wish to guide that after due consideration, the Cabinet
Secretary has approved extension of the period to carry forward
unutilised losses of Kshs 774,809,156 in relation to the financial
year.”
83. The Tribunal noted the reason given by the Respondent for
disallowing the carry forward of losses was undeclared sales which
it derived from a comparison of the Kenya Sugar Board data
against the sales in the Appellant’s returns where the variance
was treated as undeclared sales and applied to reduce the
allowable carry forward losses.
84. The Tribunal notes the Appellant’s contention that the reasons
given by the Respondent for non-approval were premised on an
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 31

erroneous variance computed by the Respondent without granting
the Appellant an opportunity to be heard on the cause of the said
variances.
85. The Tribunal further notes the Appellant’s witness’s testimony that
the Respondent erred by basing its analysis on an erroneous tax
period of one year whereas the Appellant had applied for change
of financial year as a result of which it prepared financial
statements for 6 months. Furthermore, that the Respondent relied
on figures of bagged sugar and estimated budget figures that had
been submitted by the Appellant to Kenya Sugar Board rather than
actual sales as captured in the Appellant’s management reports to
arrive at its erroneous decision. The Respondent on its part
asserted that it had followed due process and considered all
information provided by the Appellant in making recommendations
to the Cabinet Secretary for the disallowing of losses amounting to
Kshs. 1,065,214,414.00.
86. The Tribunal notes that according to the Appellant, that the initial
losses were Kshs. 6,442,015,483.00 and that following various
correspondence and meetings by parties the allowable losses were
reduced to Kshs. 1,840,023,570.00. The Tribunal observed that
upon application for approval to utilize tax losses beyond 10 years,
the Respondent conducted what it referred to as an analysis of
production and sales data which informed its recommendation to
the Cabinet Secretary to approve only the sum of 774,809,156.00.
From the pleadings it is clear to the Tribunal that the Respondent
made its recommendation to the Cabinet Secretary was based on
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 32

an internal analysis of data that had been provided for regulatory
purposes and not actual trading data which would have easily
been obtained from the Appellant.
87. The Tribunal observes that the Respondent upon realizing
variances in the production and sales data proceeded to make a
recommendation therefrom for disallowing of losses amounting to
Kshs. 1,065,214,414.00 without affording the Appellant an
opportunity to explain and / or reconcile the variances. The
Respondent’s actions in this regard amounted to condemning the
Appellant unheard. The principles of natural justice and fair
administrative action demand that a party should not be
condemned unheard.
88. The Appellant tabled before the Tribunal data and explanations
that demonstrate that the averred undeclared sales were indeed
variances that arose from use of erroneous comparatives,
projected estimates rather than actual trading results. While the
Respondent used data provided by the Appellant corroborated
with data from the Kenya Sugar Board, the Respondent clearly
misinterpreted the data and by failing to seek clarifications from
the Appellant made an erroneous finding that there existed under
declared sales.
89. The Tribunal finds guidance in the finding in Law Society of
Kenya v Attorney General & 2 others (2022) eKLR, where the
Court when faced with a similar matter where erroneous
information was used in rejecting an application held as follow:
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 33

“.. it is the Court’s position that There was need for the
Respondents to come out clearly and respond to each of the
grounds made in support of the application. The Petitioner was,
therefore, entitled to a well-reasoned response as to why its
application was rejected. The response left the Petitioner at
cross-roads. The Petitioner was not able to point out the
inadequacy of the grounds it tendered in support of the
application. Further, the Respondents did not request more
information to close the gap of inadequacy.”
90. It follows that the decision for disallowing carry forward of losses
was premised on conclusions drawn from the Respondent’s data
analysis that was premised on misapplication and/or
misinterpreted of data from the Appellant and the Kenya Sugar
Board. Such a misinformed decision cannot be justified.
91. Consequently, the Tribunal finds that the Respondent having failed
to authenticate the existence of undeclared sales was not justified
in issuing its decision as contained in the letter dated 29th
September 2022.
FINAL DECISION
92. For the reasons set out above, the Tribunal finds the following to
be the Orders that lend themselves:
(a) The Respondents decision dated 29th September, 2022 be and
is hereby set aside.
(b) The Respondent to review in the next 60 days, the Appellant’s
Application dated 24th July 2019 taking into consideration the
JUDGMENT – TAT NO.1367 OF 2022 TRANSMARA SUGAR COMPANY LIMITED VS. COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 34

information provided by the Appellant in compliance with the
provisions of the law.
(c) Each party is to bear its own costs.
93. It is so Ordered.
DATED and DELIVERED at NAIROBI this 27th day of March, 2026.
